contest.title: 2024-2025 ICPC Asia Jakarta Regional Contest (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Read the complete BDFS pseudocode, K range, and graph size and simplicity constraints.
Official Jakarta analysis; confirms root-merge additivity, the impossible odd values, the x(x+3)-2 sparse gadget, and greedy residual reduction.
Problem author's derivation; confirms the descending-neighbor recursive-DFS equivalence and the ancestor-neighbor times degree formula.
Read the independent single-chain construction and published code; cross-checked the staged chord increments 5,7,9,... and the value-5/value-2 residual patches.
Independently proved the odd lower bound 11, derived the fan-gadget contribution, and checked the alternative construction against the DFS-tree formula.

annotation.summary: Given K, construct a bounded undirected simple graph whose buggy stack-based DFS scans adjacency entries exactly K times, or prove that no such graph exists.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= K <= 1e9; the output must have 1 <= N <= 32768 and 1 <= M <= 65536, with no loops or parallel edges. The construction must encode a value far larger than its output size.
annotation.core_idea: Restrict attention to the component of root r=1. Because neighbors are pushed in ascending order onto a LIFO stack, first-pop behavior is exactly recursive DFS with neighbors examined in descending label order. Let T be this ordered DFS tree and let a(v) be the number of ancestors of nonroot v adjacent to v. Every such ancestor pushes v before its first pop, and no vertex can be pushed after its first pop, so v is popped exactly a(v) times. Hence k(G)=deg(r)+sum_{v!=r}a(v)deg(v)=2m+sum_{v!=r}(a(v)-1)deg(v). If two graphs have only their roots identified, DFS finishes one root branch at a time and all nonroot degrees and ancestor counts stay local, so their k-values add. A single edge has value 2. If k(G) is odd, the overhead formula contains an odd term: some v has odd deg(v)>=3 and a(v)>=2. Two ancestor neighbors plus a third incident edge force at least four edges, so k(G)>=2*4+3=11. This is attained by a triangle r-a-b-r with one pendant edge at a and labels forcing DFS r->b->a->leaf. Therefore exactly 1,3,5,7,9 are impossible, while every even value and every odd value at least 11 is representable. For a compact construction, define G_x using x local vertices a_1,...,a_x: force the DFS-tree chain r->a_x->...->a_1 by increasing labels, then connect leaf/hub a_1 to every ancestor except its parent. It has 2x-1 edges and value f(x)=x^2+3x-2: the hub contributes x^2, a_2 contributes 2, each a_i for i>=3 contributes 3, and the root contributes 2, with G_1 interpreted as one edge. For remaining target d>=13, greedily attach the largest G_x satisfying f(x)<=d-11. Since f(x+1)-f(x)=2x+4, the new residual is O(sqrt(d)); repeated square-root shrinkage makes the total number of added vertices O(sqrt(K)). Finish residual 11 with the fixed odd gadget and any even residual with value-2 edge gadgets. Every G_x uses fewer than two edges per new vertex, and the official bound keeps N<=32768 and M<=65536 for K<=1e9.
annotation.complexity: O(sqrt(K)) construction time and output space for the official greedy gadget decomposition; the emitted graph satisfies N <= 32768 and M <= 65536.
tricks: replace duplicate stack pops by degree-weighted contributions
identify the ordered DFS tree induced by ascending pushes and a LIFO stack
merge roots so independently traversed gadgets add their k-values
encode a quadratic value with a DFS-chain fan using only linear vertices and edges
greedily reduce the residual from d to O(sqrt(d))
derive the smallest odd value from 2m plus duplicate-pop overhead
review_risks: The local labels must force the chain r,a_x,...,a_1; arbitrary relabeling can change the ordered DFS tree and its value.
Root composition identifies only the starting vertices; all nonroot gadget vertices must remain disjoint.
G_1 is a one-edge special case, avoiding a duplicated root-hub edge in the general edge recipe.

solution_variants.name: additive-quadratic-fan-gadgets
solution_variants.summary: Official construction. Express K as a sum of root-glued gadget values f(x)=x^2+3x-2 by repeatedly taking the largest value that leaves at least 11, then finish with the fixed value-11 gadget and value-2 edges. Each fan forces a chain DFS tree whose leaf is adjacent to all ancestors, making both its pop multiplicity and degree equal x.

solution_variants.name: single-backbone-staged-chords
solution_variants.summary: Independent construction. Start from one descending-label DFS chain with base value 2N-2. For a fresh deep hub, add chords to successive higher-label ancestors while preserving the chain; its j-th chord raises the value by 2j+3, so q chords add q(q+4). Repeat on fresh hubs to shrink the residual, use the chord (1,N-1) for a value-5 parity correction, and attach root leaves worth 2 each.
solution_variants.complexity: O(N+M) construction time with N <= 32768 and M <= 65536

tags.evidence: The decisive structure is the exact descending-label recursive DFS tree induced by the buggy ascending-push stack; both constructions force this tree to be a chain so ancestor edges control repeated pops.
The solution depends on k(G)=deg(1)+sum a(v)deg(v), where a(v) counts adjacent DFS ancestors; this invariant proves feasibility, root-merge additivity, and every gadget value.
The task requires an explicit simple graph, and the main solution emits root-glued fan, odd-base, and edge gadgets whose proven contributions sum to K.
Choosing the largest f(x) that leaves at least 11 reduces each residual to O(sqrt(d)) and keeps the graph within the strict vertex bound.
The target is repeatedly decomposed into one quadratic gadget and a much smaller residual, with all pieces joined at the common root while preserving additivity.
The 2m-plus-overhead formula forces any odd value to be at least 11; fan and edge gadgets are even, while one value-11 gadget handles every feasible odd target.
